title: 第 62 節
en: with Megasthenes ; for he also states that the laws of the Indians were not reduced to writing.1 And Megasthenes agrees with Nearchus ; for he also shows himself perfectly acquainted with the fact that the Indians used letters for inscriptions on milestones, indicating the resting-places and distances.2 Nothing could offer a stronger confirmation of our opinion that the Indians had become acquainted with the art of writing during the Sutra period and before the conquest of Alexander, but that the)7 abstained from using it for literary purposes, than this apparent contradiction in the accounts of Nearchus and Megasthenes. Curtius, differing from Nearchus, maintains that they wrote on the soft rind of trees 3, a custom which we saw preserved in the play of Urvasi. We can hardly believe that the Indians could have used skins for writing. And, though Nicolaus Damascenus declares that he saw the ambassadors of Porus presented to Caesar Augustus in Antiochia, and that they brought a letter written kv §L(j)0epa, we must remember that this letter was written in Greek4, and that the word hi^Oepa may have been used for paper in general.5 We shall not be able to trace the Indian alphabet back much beyond Alexander's invasion. It existed, however, before Alexander. This we know from 1 Strabo, xv. 66. : NiapxpQ ce nept twv aotyiaT&v ovrio Xtyei' tovq fjiev vojxovq aypcapovg elvat.
2 Ol ayopardfxoi . . . bloTroiovcri, Kcti Kara $EKa aracia otZ/X^j', TiQiaai rag EKrpoirag Ka\ to. Ztaar^fxara drjXovaae.
3 Curtius, 8, 9. " Libri arborum teneri, haud secus quara charts literarum notas ©apiunt."
4 Strabo, xv. 73. Tijv £t ETnaroXi]v eXXtjiu^elp ev fotydipa yt~ •ypafiftipqv* 5 Herodotus, v. 58.

THE INTRODUCTION OF WRITING. 517 Nearchus himself, who ascribes to the Indians the art of making paper from cotton. Now, in looking for traces of writing before Alexander's time, we find in the Lalita-vistara, which contains the life of Buddha, that the young Sakya is represented as learning to write. Though the Lalita-vistara cannot be regarded as a contemporaneous witness, it is nevertheless a canonical book of the Buddhists, and, as such, must be ascribed to the third council. It was translated into Chinese 76 a. d. As we have seen, before, the system of instruction practised in the lecturerooms of the Brahmans, it will perhaps be of interest to glance at the schools in which Buddha was educated, or supposed to have been educated.

" When the young prince had grown, he was led to the writing-school (lipisala).1 We may leave out all the wonderful things that happened on this occasion, how he received a hundred thousand blessings, how he was surrounded by ten thousand children, preceded by ten thousand chariots full of sweetmeats, of silver and gold ; how the town of Kapilavastu was cleansed, how music sounded everywhere, and showers of flowers were poured from the roofs, windows, and balconies ; how, not satisfied with this, celestial ladies walked before him to clear the road, and the daughters of the wind scattered celestial flowers, besides other fabulous beings who all came to honour the Bodhisatva as he went to school. These marginal illustrations may be dropped in all Buddhist books, though they leave but little room for the text. When 1 Lalita-Vistara, Adhyaya, x. This work has lately been edited and partially translated by Babu Rajendralal Mitra, one of the most distinguished Sanskrit scholars in India.

Buddha entered the school, Visvamitra, the schoolmaster (darakacharya), unable to bear the majesty of the Bodhisatva' s presence, fell to the ground, and had to be lifted up by an angel, named Subhanga. After the king Suddhodana and his suite had left, the nurses and attendants sat down, and the Bodhisatva took a leaf to write on (lipiphalaka) made of sandal- wood (uragasarachandana-mayam). He then asked Visvamitra what writing he was going to teach him. Here follow sixty- four names, apparently names of alphabets,1 all of which the Bodhisatva is acquainted with, whereas Visvamitra is obliged to confess his ignorance. Nevertheless the Bodhisatva stays at school, and learns to write, together with ten thousand boys.2 1 The most interesting names are Anga (Bhagalpur), Banga (Bengal), Magadha, Dravida, Dakshina (Dekhan), Darada, Khasya (Cassia hills), China (Chinese), Huna, Deva (Devanagari), Bhaumadeva (Brahman), Uttarakurus, anudruta (cursive).

2 The following passage from the Evangelium Infantise (ed. Kike, p. 143.) offers a curious parallel : " Eratporro Hierosolymis quidam Zachrcus nomine, qui juventutem erudiebat. Dicebat hie Josepho : Quare non mittis ad me Jesum, ut literas discat ? Annuebat illi Josephus, et ad Divam Mariam hoc referebat. Ad magistrum itaque ilium ducebant ; qui simulatque eum conspexerat Alphabetum ipsi conscripsit, utque Aleph diceret praecepit. Et eum dixisset Aleph, magister ipsum Beth pronunciare jubebat. Cui Dominus Jesus : Die mihi prius significationem literae Aleph, et turn Beth pronunciabo. Cumque magister verbera ipsi intentaret, exponebat illi Dominus Jesus significationes literarum Aleph et Beth ; item, quamam literarum figurae essent rectae, qusenam obliqua3, quaenam duplicatae, qua? punctis insignitaa, qua? iisdem carentes ; quare una litera aliam precederet; aliaque plurima enarrare ccepit et elucidare, quae magister ipse nee audiverat unquam nee in libro ullo legerat. Dixit porro magistro Dominus Jesus : Attende, ut dicam tibi, coepitque clare et distincte recitare, Aleph, Beth, Gimel, Daleth, usque ad finem 41phabeti. Quod miratus magister, Hunc, inquit, puerum ante Noachum THE INTRODUCTION OF WRITING. 519 The alphabet which he learns is the common Sanskrit alphabet, with the omission of the letters 1, ri, and ri. It consists of 45 letters, and, as in our own primers, every letter is followed by a word containing that letter at the beginning or in the middle. These words in the Lalita-vistara are so chosen as to illustrate some of the chief points of Buddha's own doctrines. The alphabet is : — a, a, i, i, u, u, e, ai, o, au, am, ah ; k, kh, g, gh, n ; ch, chh, j, jh, n ; t, th, d, dh, n; t, th, d, dh, n; p, ph, b, bh, m; y, r, v; s, sh, s, h, ksh.

Though the further education of Buddha is not fully described, we see him soon afterwards, in a general competition, the most distinguished scholar, arithmetician, musician, and everything else.1 This comprehensive system of education, through which Buddha is here represented to have passed, is the very opposite of that followed by the Brahmans. We nowhere meet in the Buddhist literature with those strong imprecations against book-learning which we found among the Brahmans, and which may be heard, I believe, even at the present day.

If, thus, the first, though rather legendary, trace of writing, as a part of the elementary education in India, is 2 discovered in the life of Buddha, it is curious to natura esse existimo ; conversusque ad Josephum, Adduxisti, ait, ad me erudiendum puerum, raagistris omnibus doctiorem. Divae quoque Mariae inquit : Filio tuo nulla doctrina opus est." The Gospel of Thomas the Israelite, or the Book of Thomas the Israelite, the philosopher, concerning the acts which the Lord did, when a child, was most popular in the east.
1 Among the subjects in which he shows his learning, figure Nirghantu, Nigama, Purana, Itihasa, Veda, Vyakarana, Nirukta, 6iksha, Chhandas, Kalpa, Jyotisha, Sankhya, Yoga, Vaiseshika.
2 In an ancient inscription of Khandgiri (Journal of the Asiat.
l, i. 4 520 THE INTRODUCTION OF WRITING.

observe that the first actual writing, the first well authenticated inscription in India, is likewise of Buddhist origin. There are no Brahmanic inscriptions earlier than the Buddhist inscriptions of Asoka on the rocks of Kapurdigiri, Dhauli, and Girnar. They belong to the third century before Christ. They call themselves lipi, a writing !, or dharmalipi 2, a sacred writing ; and they mention the writer or engraver by the name of lipikara3 This last word lipikara is an important word, for it is the only word in the Sutras of Panini which can be legitimately adduced to prove that Panini was acquainted with the art of writing. He teaches the formation of this word, iii. 2, 21. There is indeed another passage, which has frequently been quoted, where Panini teaches the formation of the adjective yavanani. This is simply the feminine of yavana, as Indrdni is of Indra. Katyayana, however, and the Commentator, both maintain that yavandni is used as a name of lipi, and that it meant the writing of the Yavanas. I see no reason to doubt that most of the examples which we find in the Commentaries go back to the very time of Panini, and I am quite willing to admit that Panini gave his rule on yavanani simply in order to explain this word as the name of a certain alphabet. But I must demur to Soc. of Bengal, vi. 318.), a king is mentioned who in his youth learned to write, and was taught, besides, arithmetic, navigation, commerce, and law (" tato likharupagana nava vyapara vidhi visaradena ").
1 Etaya athaya iyam lipi likhita ; for this purpose was the writing written.
2 Iyam dhammalipi Devanam piyena piyadasina rana likhapita asti eva. (p. 752.)
3 Burnouf, Lotus, p. 752.

THE INTRODUCTION OF WRITING. 521 any further conclusions. Yavana is by no means the exclusive name of the Greeks or Ionians. Professor Lassen has proved that it had a much wider meaning, and that it was even used of Semitic nations. There is nothing to prove that Panini was later than Alexander, or that he was acquainted with Greek literature. In the Lalita-vistara, where all possible alphabets are mentioned, nothing is said of a Yavanani or a Greek alphabet. The Sanskrit alphabet, though it has always been suspected to be derived from a Semitic source, has not certainly been traced back to a Greek source. It shows more similarity with the Aramasan than with any other variety of the Phoenician alphabet.1 Yavanani lipi most likely means that variety of the Semitic alphabet which, previous to Alexander, and previous to Panini, became the type of the Indian alphabet. But all this is merely conjectural. It is impossible to arrive at any certain interpretation of Yavanani, as used by Panini ; and it is much better to confess this, than to force the word into an argument for any preconceived notions as to the origin of the Indian alphabet.

There is another word in Panini which might seem to prove that, not only the art of writing, but written books were known at his time. This is grantha. Grantha occurs four times in our texts of Panini.2 In I. 3, 1 Lepsius, Zwei sprachvergleichende Abhandlungen, p. 78., Schulze's conjecture about Mesnud. Weber, Indische Skizzen.

75. it is so used as to apply to the Veda. In IV. 3, 87. it may refer to any work. In IV. 3, 116. it is applied to the work of any individual author. In VI. 3, 79. it may refer to any work that is studied. I do not attribute much importance to the fact that I. 3. 75. and IV. 3, 116. are marked as not explained in the Commentaries ; for I confess that in none of these four passages can I discover anything to prove that graniha must mean a written or a bound book. Grantha is derived from a root gratJi, which means nectere, severe. Grantha, therefore, like the later sandarbha, would simply mean a composition.1 It corresponds etymologically with the Latin textus. Thus it is used by the Commentator to Nir. I. 20., where he says that former teachers handed down the hymns granthato 'rthatascha, " according to their text and according to their meaning." In the later literature of India grantha was used for a volume, and in granthaMtt, a library, we see clearly that it lias that meaning. But in the early literature grantha does not mean jmstaka, or book ; it means simply a composition, as opposed to a traditional work.

This distinction between traditional works and works composed by individual authors is of frequent occurrence in Panini, and we attempted, in a former part of this work, to draw some historical conclusions from this distinction. From IV. 3, 101. to 111. the grammarian gives rules how to derive the titles of works from the names of those by whom they were proclaimed (tena proktam). But in most cases these derivations are used by Panini as intermediate links 1 Thus the Commentator to the Rig-veda, 1, 67, 4. explains chritanti by agnim uddisya stutir grathnanti, kurvantityarthah.

THE INTRODUCTION OF WRITING. 523 only, in order to form the names of Charanas who read and preserve these works. Never, he says (IV. 2, titi.), use the derivative, which would be the title of a work, in the case of hymns (chhandas^ or Brahmanas. Do not call a work proclaimed by Katha, Katham, but only speak of Kathas, L e. those who hand down the works proclaimed by Katha. Another still more significant restriction is made by Panini. With reference to modern works, he says, you may use the neuter in the singular or plural, instead of the plural of the masculine. The Brahmanas taught by Yajnavalkya may be spoken of as such. But the ancient Brahmanas, first proclaimed by Bhallava &c, can only be spoken of as "the Bhallavins" (Bhallavida3), because it is only in the tradition of his descendants that the works of Bhallava and other ancient sages may be said to live. However we examine the ancient Sanskrit phraseology with regard to books and their authors, we invariably arrive at the same results. In the most ancient literature, the idea even of authorship is excluded. Yvrorks are spoken of as revealed to and communicated by certain sages, but not as composed by them. In the later literature of the Brahmana and Sutra period the idea of authorship is admitted, but no trace is to be found anywhere of any books being committed to writing. It is possible I may have overlooked some words in the Brahmanas and Sutras, which would prove the existence of written books previous to Panini. If so, it is not from any wish to suppress them. I believe, indeed, that the Brahmanas were preserved by oral tradition only, but I should feel inclined to claim an acquaintance with the art of writing for the authors of the Sutras. And there is 524 THE INTRODUCTION OF WRITING.

one word which seems to strengthen such a supposition. We find that several of the Sutras are divided into chapters called patalas. This is a word never used for the subdivisions of the Brahraanas. Its meaning is a covering, the surrounding skin or membrane ; it is also used for a tree. If so, it would seem to be almost synonymous with liber and 0/#?vo£, and it would mean book, after meaning originally a sheet of paper made of the surrounding bark of trees. If writing came in towards the latter half of the Sutra period, it would no doubt be applied at the same time to reducing the hymns and Brahmanas to a written form. Previously to that time, however, we are bound to maintain that the collection of the hymns, and the immense mass of the Brahmana literature, were preserved by means of oral tradition only.

The three periods of Yedic literature which we have examined, the Sutra, Brahmana, and Mantra periods, all point to some earlier age which gave birth to the poetry of the early Rishis. There was a time, doubtless, when the songs which were collected with such careful zeal in the Mantra period, commented upon with such elaborate pedantry during the Brahmana period, and examined and analysed with such minute exactness during the Sutra period, lived and were understood without any effort by a simple and pious race. There was a time when the sacrifices, which afterwards became so bewildering a system of ceremonies, were dictated by the free impulse of the human heart, by a yearning to render thanks to some Unknown Being, and to repay, in words or deeds, a debt of gratitude, accumulated from the first breath of life. There was a time when the poet was the leader, the king, and priest of his family or tribe, when his songs and sayings were listened to in anxious silence and with implicit faith, when his prayers were repeated by crowds who looked up to their kings and priests, their leaders and judges, as men better, nobler, wiser than the rest, as beings nearer to the gods in proportion as they were raised above 526 THE CIIIIANDAS PERIOD.

the common level of mankind. These men themselves living a life of perfect freedom, speaking a language not yet broken by literary usage, and thinking thoughts unfettered as yet by traditional chains, were at once teachers, lawgivers, poets, and priests. There is no very deep wisdom in their teaching, their laws are simple, their poetry shows no very high flights of fancy, and their religion might be told in a few words. But what there is of their language, poetry, and religion has a charm which no other period of Indian literature possesses : it is spontaneous, original, and truthful.

We cannot say this of all the hymns: nay, the greater portion of what we now possess of Yedic poetry must, no doubt, be ascribed to a secondary period, the socalled Mantra period. But after we have discarded what bears the stamp of a later age, there remains enough to give us an idea of an earlier race of Yedic poets. It is true, no doubt, in one sense, that even those earliest specimens of Yedic poetry belong, as has been said by Bunsen, to the modern history of the human race. Ages must have passed before the grammatical texture of the Yedic Sanskrit could have assumed the consistency and regularity which it shows throughout. Every tense, every mood, every number and person of the verb is fixed, and all the terminations of the cases are firmly established. Every one of these terminations was originally an independent word with an independent meaning. Their first selection was more or less the result of individual choice, their technical character the result of long usage. There was more than one word for i, and more than one expression for the verb to be. The selection of mi% as the termination of the first person ANTECEDENT ELEMENTS. 527 singular, the selection of as in the sense of to be, and the joining of the two so as to produce the auxiliary verb asmi, I am, all this was a conventional act, the act of one or two individuals, fixed by circumstances which were more or less accidental. If, then, we find the same combination in the ancient Greek la-^i, and the modern Lithuanian esrai, it is clear that the origin of that form goes back to times long anterior to the separate existence of Sanskrit, Greek, and Lithuanian. As soy, suisy and sono are modern modifications that point back to an earlier type, the Latin sum, the Sanskrit asmi, Greek e<rp, Lithuanian esmi, are likewise but the modern representatives of some earlier typical form, which existed in the undivided language of the Aryan race.

The same applies to the religion of the Yeda. Words like deva for 'god' mark a more than secondary stage in the grammar of the Aryan religion. To use the root div, ' to shine,' with reference to the heavenly bodies, was the result of a free choice. There were other roots which might have been used instead. Nor was it by any means a necessity that the presence of a Divine Power should be felt exclusively in the bright manifestations of nature. All this was the result of a historical growth ; and the early periods of that growth had passed away long before the Rishis of India could have worshipped their JDevas or their bright beings, with sacred hymns and invocations.

From this point of view the Vedic language and poetry may be ascribed to a modern or secondary period in the history of the world, if only it be understood that what preceded that period in India, or in any other part of the Aryan world, is lost to us beyond the hope of recovery, and that, therefore, to us the 528 ANTECEDENT ELEMENTS.

Veda represents the most ancient chapter in the history of the human intellect. We find no traces in the Yeda, or in any Aryan work, of a growing language, growing in the sense in which some of the Turanian languages may be said to be still growing at the present day.1 The whole grammatical mechanism is finished, the most complicated forms are sanctioned, and the only changes of which the Aryan speech, arrived at the point where we find it in the Yeda, admits, are those of gradual decay and recomposition. Nor do we find any traces, in the Yeda, of a growing religion. We look in vain for the effect produced on the human mind by the first rising of the idea of God. To the poets of the Yeda that idea is an old and familiar idea : it is understood, never questioned, never denied. We shall never hear what was felt by man when the image of God arose in all its majesty before his eyes, assuming a reality before which all other realities faded away into a mere shadow. No whisper will ever reach us of that sacred colloquy when God for the first time spoke to man, and man to God ; when man within his own heart heard that still small voice through which the Father of mankind revealed himself to all his children, to the Jew first, and also to the Gentile ; and when God received the first response from human lips : " Who art thou, Lord ? " That first recognition of God, that first perception of the real presence of God, — a perception without which no religion, whether natural or revealed, can exist or grow, — belonged to the past when the songs of the Yeda were written. The idea of God, 1 See my Letter on the Classification of the Turanian languages, p. 30.

ANTECEDENT ELEMENTS. 529 though never entirely lost, had been clouded over by errors. The names given to God had been changed to gods, and their real meaning had faded away from the memory of man. Even the earliest hymns of the Veda are not free from mythological phraseology. How far the poets retained a vague consciousness of the original purport of the names of the gods is difficult to say. To our eyes the science of language has disclosed the smallest fibres in the tissue of these names, and allowed us an insight into the darkest secrets of their growth. We can see nomina, where even the most keen-sighted native could discover nothing but numina. Sometimes, however, we feel surprised at the precision with which even such modern writers as Kumarila are able to read the true meaning of their mythology. When Kumarila is hard pressed by his opponents about the immoralities of his gods, he answers with all the freedom of a comparative mycologist l : " It is fabled that Prajapati, the Lord of Creation, did violence to his daughter. But what does it mean ? Prajapati, the Lord of Creation, is a name of the sun ; and he is called so, because he pro- M M 530 THE CHHANDAS PERIOD.
zh: 與美加斯提尼斯（Megasthenes）一致；因為他也聲稱印度人的法律並未被記錄成文字。1 而美加斯提尼斯與尼阿爾庫斯相符；因為他同樣表明自己對印度人在里程碑上使用字母以標示休息地點和距離這一事實有完全的認識。2 沒有什麼能比尼阿爾庫斯與美加斯提尼斯記載中的這種表面矛盾更強有力地證實我們的看法：印度人在蘇特羅時期及亞歷山大征服之前已經熟悉文字藝術，但他們卻避免將之用於文學目的。3 庫爾提烏斯與尼阿爾庫斯不同，主張印度人在樹皮的柔軟內層上書寫，這是我們在《烏爾瓦希》的戲劇中看到保存下來的習俗。我們幾乎無法相信印度人會使用獸皮來書寫。而且，雖然尼古勞斯‧大馬士革人宣稱他看到波魯斯的使節被呈獻給凱撒‧奧古斯都在安條克，且他們攜帶了用 βίβλος（bíblos）書寫的信件，我們必須記得這封信是用希臘文書寫的4，而 βίβλος 這個詞可能被用來泛指紙張。5 我們將不能將印度字母溯源到亞歷山大入侵之前太遠。然而，它確實存在於亞歷山大之前。我們由以下事實知曉1 斯特拉波，第十五卷，66：「尼阿爾庫斯關於智者這樣說：他們的法律是未成文的。」
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」實際上是古希臘文，而非英文。根據您的指示，我應該只翻譯英文原文。 這段文本是古希臘文，不在翻譯範圍內。 請提供英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行繁體中文翻譯。
3 Curtius, 8, 9. 「樹皮書籍柔軟，恰如紙張承載文字記號。」
4 斯特拉波，《地理志》第十五卷第七十三章。Τὴν δὲ Ἐπταπολίην ἑλληνίζειν ἐν τοῖς χρόνοις ἐκείνοις.* 5 希羅多德，《歷史》第五卷第五十八章。

517 尼阿爾科斯本人就認為印度人具有從棉花製造紙張的技術。現在，在尋找亞歷山大時代之前的文字痕跡時，我們在《佛陀生平經》（Lalita-vistara）中發現，年輕的釋迦族人被描繪成正在學習書寫。雖然《佛陀生平經》不能被視為同時代的見證，但它畢竟是佛教的正典之書，因此必須將其歸於第三次結集。它在公元76年被譯成中文。如前所述，在瞥一眼佛陀受教育或據說受過教育的學校之前，我們已經看到了婆羅門講堂所採行的教學體系，這也許會很有興趣。

當王子長大成人時，他被帶往書法學校（lipisala）。¹我們可以省略此際發生的所有奇異事跡，例如他獲得十萬次祝福、被一萬名兒童環繞、由十萬輛裝載糖果、白銀和黃金的車隊引導；迦毘羅衛城如何被清潔，四處音樂聲聲入耳，從屋頂、窗戶和陽臺傾瀉而下的花雨；還有天界仕女在他面前行走以清掃道路，風之女兒散落天界之花，以及其他種種虛幻的生靈，都前來尊敬即將上學的菩薩（Bodhisatva）。這些邊欄插圖在所有佛教經典中都可以刪除，雖然它們幾乎沒有留下篇幅給正文。¹ 1 Lalita-Vistara, Adhyaya, x. 此書最近由印度最傑出的梵文學者之一巴布‧拉傑德拉拉爾‧米特拉（Babu Rajendralal Mitra）編訂並部分翻譯。

佛陀進入學堂，校師毗濕婆蜜多羅（Visvamitra）因無法承受菩薩威嚴的臨在，跌倒在地，必須由一位名喚善顏（Subhanga）的天使扶起。待國王淨飯王（Suddhodana）及其隨從離去後，保母與侍者坐下，菩薩取一片檀木製成的書籤葉片（lipiphalaka，uragasarachandana-mayam）來書寫。隨後他詢問毗濕婆蜜多羅打算教他何種文字。接著列出六十四個名稱，表面上乃各種字母的名稱¹，菩薩悉知其詳，而毗濕婆蜜多羅卻不得不承認自己無知。儘管如此，菩薩仍留在學堂，與一萬名男童一起學習書寫。² ¹ 最有趣的名稱包括：憍薩羅國（Anga‧Bhagalpur）、孟加拉（Banga‧Bengal）、摩揭陀（Magadha）、德拉威達（Dravida）、南方（Dakshina‧德干）、達羅陀（Darada）、喀什亞（Khasya‧卡西亞丘陵）、中國（China‧Chinese）、匈奴（Huna）、天城體（Deva‧Devanagari）、梵天（Bhaumadeva‧Brahman）、北俱盧洲（Uttarakurus）、草書體（anudruta‧cursive）。

2 《嬰幼耶穌福音書》（Evangelium Infantise）第143頁（基克版）中的以下段落提供了一個奇妙的對應：「昔有耶路撒冷人名撒迦利亞者，以教導青年為業。此人謂約瑟曰：何不遣耶穌就我學文字乎？約瑟許之，並將此事告知聖母瑪利亞。遂將幼童送往其師所；師初見之，即為其記寫字母，令其誦讀阿列夫（Aleph）。幼童既誦阿列夫，師令其誦貝特（Beth）。主耶穌言曰：先請告我阿列夫之義，然後我誦貝特。師將鞭杖加於童子，主耶穌乃為其闡明阿列夫與貝特之義；又說明諸字之形孰為直、孰為斜、孰為重複、孰以點號著、孰無點號；又說明何以一字先於他字；並更述說及闡明其師從未聞聞、亦未在任何書中所讀之眾多他事。主耶穌復語其師曰：汝聽，我今為汝言之，遂清晰分明地誦讀曰：阿列夫、貝特、吉美爾（Gimel）、達列特（Daleth），以至字母之終。」師見而異之，遂言：此童子…… **書寫的引入** 519 彼之習學之字母乃常用梵文字母，惟省略 ri、ri 二字。共計四十五字母，如同我等之識字課本，每一字母之後，繼以一詞，其詞之首或中間含有該字母。《樂瓔珞莊嚴經》（Lalita-vistara）中之諸詞悉為精心選擇，以闡明佛陀本身教義之諸要點。其字母如下： a、ā、i、ī、u、ū、e、ai、o、au、am、ah；k、kh、g、gh、ṅ；ch、chh、j、jh、ñ；ṭ、ṭh、ḍ、ḍh、ṇ；t、th、d、dh、n；p、ph、b、bh、m；y、r、v；s、ś、ṣ、h、kṣ。
雖然佛陀的深造教育並未被完整描述，但我們隨即看到他在一場普遍的競賽中嶄露頭角，成為最傑出的學者、算術家、音樂家，及其他各方面的翹楚。1 這套讓佛陀據說經歷過的綜合教育體系，與婆羅門人所遵循的教育方式恰恰相反。在佛教文獻中，我們找不到婆羅門人那種對於書本學問的強烈詛咒，而那種詛咒我們曾經發現過，我相信時至今日仍然能聽聞。

如果說，印度初等教育中最早雖然帶有傳說色彩的書寫痕跡，是在佛陀的生平中被發現的，那麼值得注意的是，早期基督教文獻中關於耶穌童年教育的記載，也呈現出類似的特徵。正如一份古老的文獻所載：「自然認為他應當受教；遂轉身向約瑟夫說，『你把這孩子帶來教導我，他比所有的教師都更有學問。』又對聖母瑪麗亞說：『你的兒子不需要任何教導。』」《以色列人托馬斯福音》，或稱《以色列人哲學家托馬斯之書》，記述主為孩童時所行的事蹟，在東方最為流傳。

在他展現學問的眾多科目中，包括《詞彙集》（Nirghantu）、《邏輯論》（Nigama）、《往世書》（Purana）、《歷史敘事》（Itihasa）、《吠陀》（Veda）、《文法學》（Vyakarana）、《詞源學》（Nirukta）、《音韻學》（Śiksha）、《韻律學》（Chhandas）、《儀軌論》（Kalpa）、《天文學》（Jyotisha）、《數論》（Sankhya）、《瑜伽》（Yoga）、《勝論派》（Vaiseshika）。
2 在干德吉里（Khandgiri）的一份古代銘文中（亞洲學會期刊，

在文明的歷史上，很少有發明如書寫之引入般重要。書寫不僅標誌著人類智識發展的一個決定性轉折點，更使得知識得以超越個人記憶的侷限，得以永久保存與廣泛傳播。 書寫系統的發展經歷了漫長的演進過程。最初，人類採用圖畫或符號來記錄事物；隨後，這些符號逐漸演變為表音文字，最終發展出完整的字母系統。這一轉變賦予了人類前所未有的能力——將思想固定於物質載體之上，使之得以跨越時間與空間的限制。 書寫的出現深刻改變了宗教傳統的性質。在口頭傳承的時代，宗教文本依賴於記憶與覆述，因此難免遭受扭曲與遺忘。書寫的出現使得宗教經典得以精確保存，聖典內容因而獲得了前所未有的權威性與穩定性。吠陀（Veda）、奧義書（Upanishad）等重要宗教文獻的記錄，正體現了書寫對於宗教發展的根本性影響。 因此，研究宗教學者必須重視書寫引入這一歷史事件，並認識其對於人類文明與宗教傳統發展所帶來的深遠意義。

應當注意，印度最初的實際文字記載、最初經過確實驗證的銘文同樣源自佛教。在阿育王（Asoka）刻於卡普爾迪吉里（Kapurdigiri）、達烏里（Dhauli）和吉爾納（Girnar）等岩石上的佛教銘文之前，並無婆羅門教銘文。這些銘文屬於基督紀年前第三世紀。它們自稱為 lipi（文字），或 dharmalipi（神聖文字）；並以 lipikara 之名提及其撰寫者或刻字者。這個 lipikara 一詞相當重要，因為它是《帕尼尼蘇特羅經》（Sutras of Panini）中唯一能合法引用以證明帕尼尼（Panini）熟悉書寫藝術的詞彙。他在第三卷第二章第二十一節教授這個詞的形成。確實有另一段常被引述的文句，其中帕尼尼教授形容詞 yavana 的構成。這只是 yavana 的陰性形式，如 Indrani 之於 Indra。然而，迦多耶那（Katyayana）及其評釋者均主張 yavana 被用作 lipi 的名稱，並指其意為雅瓦那人（Yavanas）的文字。我看不出理由懷疑評釋中的大多數例子是否來自帕尼尼本人的年代，我也完全願意承認帕尼尼制訂 yavana 的規則，純粹是為了將這個詞解釋為某種字母的名稱。但我必須反對〔某處引文〕，一位國王在其青年時期學會了書寫，並且還學習了算術、航海、商業和法律（"tato likharupagana nava vyapara vidhi visaradena"）。
依此目的，此文字乃被書寫；為此目的而文字被書寫。
2 Iyam dhammalipi Devanam piyena piyadasina rana likhapita asti eva.（第752頁）
3 勃爾諾夫，《蓮花經》，第752頁。

任何進一步的結論。**耶跋那**（Yavana）絕非希臘人或愛奧尼亞人的專屬名稱。拉森教授已證明它具有更廣泛的含義，甚至曾用於閃語族民族。沒有任何證據表明帕尼尼晚於亞歷山大，或他熟悉希臘文學。在《妙法蓮華經》（Lalita-vistara）中，雖然提及了各種可能的字母，但未曾提及耶跋那尼字母或希臘字母。梵文字母雖然一直被懷疑源自閃語族來源，但尚未確定可追溯到希臘來源。它與亞蘭字母的相似度高於腓尼基字母的任何其他變體。¹**耶跋那尼字母**（Yavanani lipi）最可能是指那種閃語族字母的變體，在亞歷山大之前、帕尼尼之前，它成為印度字母的典範。但所有這些不過是推測而已。不可能對帕尼尼所使用的**耶跋那尼**做出任何確定的解釋；與其將這個詞強行納入關於印度字母起源的任何先入為主的觀念之中，不如坦誠承認這一點要好得多。

在帕尼尼的著作中還有另一個詞彙，似乎可以證明不僅書寫的藝術，而且已成文的書籍在他的時代就為人所知。這個詞是 grantha（書籍）。Grantha 在我們的帕尼尼文本中出現了四次。在 I. 3, 1 Lepsius, *Zwei sprachvergleichende Abhandlungen*, p. 78., Schulze 關於 Mesnud 的臆測。Weber, *Indische Skizzen*。

75. 它被用來指涉吠陀。在四‧三‧87，它可能指任何著作。在四‧三‧116，它被用於任何個別作者的著作。在六‧三‧79，它可能指任何被研習的著作。我並不認為以下事實具有重大意義：即一‧三‧75 和四‧三‧116 被註疏家標記為未作解釋；因為我必須坦白，在這四個段落中，我都找不到任何證據以證明 graniha 必定指書寫或裝訂的書籍。Grantha 衍生自詞根 gra-nth，其意義為「編綴、結合」（nectere, severe）。因此 grantha，如同後來的 sandarbha，只是指一部著作而已。¹ 它在詞源上與拉丁文的 textus 相應。如此用法見於《尼魯克塔註疏》（Nir.）一‧20，註疏家在彼處言及前代教師傳承讚歌時說「依其本文與其意義」（granthato 'rthatascha）。在印度的後期文獻中，grantha 被用來指卷冊，而在 grantha-pīṭh（文庫）一詞中，我們清楚地看到它具有該種含義。但在早期文獻中，grantha 並不意指 pus͟taka（書籍）；它只是指一部著作，與傳統著作相對立。

這種傳統著作與個人作者所作著作之間的區分在帕尼尼（Panini）著作中頻繁出現，我們曾在本著作的前面部分嘗試從這種區分中得出某些歷史結論。從第四卷第三章第 101 至 111 頌，這位文法家給出了規則，說明如何從宣布這些著作的人的名字推導著作的標題（tena proktam）。然而在多數情況下，帕尼尼使用這些推導作為中介環節 1 因此，《梨俱吠陀》的評注者在 1, 67, 4 處解釋 chritanti 為 agnim uddisya stutir grathnanti, kurvantityarthah。

僅為了形成那些誦讀並保存這些著作的婆羅門學者（Charanas）的名字而已。他說（IV. 2, titi.），絕不可使用衍生詞——在讚美詩（chhandas）或婆羅門經（Brahmanas）的情況下，這將是某部著作的標題。不要稱某部由卡塔（Katha）所誦述的著作為「卡塔姆」（Katham），而只應談及「卡塔斯」（Kathas），即那些傳承由卡塔所誦述之著作的人。帕尼尼（Panini）所作的另一項更具意義的限制是：關於現代著作，他說可以使用中性單數或複數形式，而不用陽性複數形式。由亞傑瓦爾基亞（Yajnavalkya）所教導的婆羅門經可以如此稱呼。但是由跋羅婆等古代聖賢首先誦述的古代婆羅門經，只能稱作「跋羅婆人的傳承」（Bhallavida），因為只有在其後裔的傳統中，跋羅婆及其他古代聖賢的著作才能說是活躍的。無論我們如何審視古代梵文有關書籍及其作者的措辭，我們總是不變地得出相同的結論。在最古老的文獻中，甚至「創作」的概念都被排除了。著作被說成是由某些聖賢所啟示並傳達的，但不是由他們創作的。在婆羅門經及蘇特拉（Sutra）時期的後期文獻中，創作的概念才被承認，但在任何地方都找不到任何書籍在帕尼尼之前被記錄成文字的痕跡。我可能在婆羅門經和蘇特拉中遺漏了某些詞彙，這些詞彙將證明帕尼尼之前存在著錄文字的書籍。若是如此，這並非出於任何隱瞞的意願。我確實相信婆羅門經是由口頭傳統單獨保存的，但我傾向於認為蘇特拉的作者已掌握文字的藝術。而且有

有一個詞彙似乎可以加強這樣的假設。我們發現幾部《經書》（Sutra）被分為稱為「章」（patala）的部分。這是一個從未用於《梵書》（Brahmanas）分段的詞彙。它的含義是覆蓋物，即環繞的皮膚或膜；它也用來指樹木。若果如此，它似乎幾乎與 *liber* 和 *βίβλος* 同義，意為書籍，其原義是由樹木周圍樹皮製成的紙張。如果書寫在《經書》時期的後半葉才出現，那麼毫無疑問，它會同時被應用於將讚歌和《梵書》文獻化為書面形式。然而，在此之前，我們必然要主張，讚歌的匯編以及龐大的《梵書》文獻，都是以口頭傳統的方式保存下來的。

我們所研究過的吠陀文獻三個時期——蘇特拉期（Sutra）、婆羅門期（Brahmana）和曼特拉期（Mantra）——都指向某個更早的年代，那個年代孕育了早期瑞師（Rishi）的詩歌。毫無疑問曾有一個時代，那些以如此謹慎的熱忱在曼特拉期被蒐集起來、在婆羅門期遭受如此精雕細琢的經學解釋、在蘇特拉期經歷如此細緻精確的檢視與分析的歌曲，曾被一個樸素而虔誠的民族輕而易舉地理解與傳唱。曾有一個時代，那些後來變成令人眼花繚亂的繁複儀式體系的祭祀，乃是源自人心的自由衝動、源自對某個未知之在者（Unknown Being）表達感謝的渴望，以及用言語或行為償還自生命初息起就不斷累積的感恩之債。曾有一個時代，詩人是其家族或部落的領導者、國王與祭司，他的歌曲與言論在焦急的沉默中被傾聽，以及被無條件地信賴，他的祈禱文被眾人重複誦讀——那些人仰望他們的國王與祭司、他們的領導者與法官，視之為比他人更優異、更崇高、更睿智的人物，視之為與諸神更為接近的存在，其程度正與他們被拔高於眾人的高度相應。
人類的普遍水準。這些人本身過著完全自由的生活，說著一種尚未被文學用法所破壞的語言，思想著尚未被傳統鎖鏈所束縛的念頭，他們同時是教師、立法者、詩人和祭司。他們的教導中沒有很深的智慧，他們的律法很簡單，他們的詩歌未曾展現過高超的想像力，他們的宗教可以用寥寥數語道盡。但是他們的語言、詩歌和宗教中所具有的特質，是印度文學任何其他時期都不曾擁有的：它是自發的、原創的，且是真實的。

我們不能對所有讚歌都這樣說。事實上，我們現今所擁有的吠陀詩歌絕大多數，無疑應當歸屬於一個較晚的時期，即所謂的「誦文時期」（Mantra period）。但是，當我們摒棄了那些帶有較晚時代印記的內容之後，仍然保留了足夠的材料，使我們能夠對早期的吠陀詩人種族有所認識。誠然，在某種意義上說，即便是那些最早期的吠陀詩歌標本，如班森（Bunsen）所言，也屬於人類的近代史。在吠陀梵文的文法結構能夠呈現出其所顯示的那種一致性和規則性之前，必須經歷了許多時代。動詞的每一個時態、每一個語氣、每一個數和人稱都已固定，而所有格的所有詞尾也都牢固確立。這些詞尾中的每一個原本都是一個具有獨立意義的獨立詞彙。它們最初的選用在某種程度上是個人選擇的結果，而它們的技術性特徵則是長期使用的結果。過去曾有不止一個表示「我」的詞，也不止一種表達「是」這個動詞的方式。選擇 *mi* 作為第一人稱單數的詞尾，選擇 *as* 表示「是」的意義，以及將兩者結合起來形成助動詞 *asmi*（我是），所有這一切都是一種約定俗成的行為，是由或多或少帶有偶然性質的環境所規定的一兩個人的行為。因此，如果我們在古代希臘語 *ε-ἰ-μι*（eimi）和現代立陶宛語 *esrai* 中發現同樣的組合，就很清楚該形式的起源應當追溯到梵文、希臘語和立陶宛語各自分離存在之前的年代。正如 *soy*、*suisy* 和 *sono* 是指向一個更早類型的現代變體一樣，拉丁文 *sum*、梵文 *asmi*、希臘文 *εἰμί*（eimi）、立陶宛文 *esmi* 同樣只是某個更早典型形式的現代代表，而該形式曾經存在於未分化的雅利安語族共同語言之中。

同樣的情況也適用於吠陀的宗教。像 deva（「提婆」）這樣表示「神」的詞彙，標誌著雅利安宗教在文法層次上已超越初級階段。將根詞 div（「閃耀」）用於指涉天體，乃是自由抉擇的結果。當時可以選用的根詞還有其他許多。同樣地，神聖力量的顯現必然專專地落現於自然界的光明表現，這絕非不可或缺之事。這一切都是歷史生長的結果；而在印度的瑞詩（Rishis）能夠以聖歌和祈禱詞來崇拜他們的提婆或光明之靈之前，那個生長過程的早期階段早已消逝已久。
從這個觀點看來，吠陀的語言與詩歌可被歸屬於世界歷史上的一個現代或次生時期，只要我們明白在印度或雅利安世界任何其他地區先於此時期而存在的東西，對我們而言已經永遠失落，無有復得的希望，因此對我們來說，528 先行的要素。

吠陀代表了人類理智歷史中最遠古的篇章。我們在吠陀中，或在任何雅利安著作中，都找不到一種成長中的語言的痕跡——這種成長的意義是指某些圖蘭語族可以說在當今仍在成長的那種方式。整個語法機制已經完成，最複雜的語法形式已經得到認可，而雅利安語言在我們於吠陀中發現它的那一點所允許的唯一變化，是那些逐漸衰退與重組的變化。我們也在吠陀中找不到一種成長中的宗教的痕跡。我們徒勞地尋找人類心靈因上帝觀念的首次升起而產生的影響。對於吠陀的詩人們來說，這個觀念是一個古老而熟悉的觀念：它是被理解的、從未被質疑的、從未被否認的。我們永遠不會聽到當上帝的形象在人眼前以其威嚴升起之時、當它之前所有其他現實都褪成了單純的陰影時，人類所感受到的是什麼。當上帝首次向人類說話，人也向上帝說話之時，當人在自己的心中聽到那微小的聲音、人類之父藉此向他的所有兒女——先向猶太人，也向非猶太人——啟示自己之時，當上帝從人類的嘴唇中首次得到回應：「主啊，你是誰？」——這聖潔的對話沒有一絲隻言片語會傳到我們耳中。那對上帝的首次認識，那對上帝真實臨在的首次感知——沒有這種感知，無論是自然宗教還是啟示宗教都無法存在或成長——都屬於吠陀歌曲被寫作時代之前的過去。上帝的觀念，1 1 參見我論圖蘭語族分類的信函，第三十頁。

雖然從未完全喪失，但已被種種謬誤所籠罩。賦予神的名號已被改作眾神，其真實意義已從人類的記憶中褪去。即使是吠陀最古老的讚歌也未能免於神話詞彙。詩人們在多大程度上保持著對神名原初旨意的模糊意識，實難判斷。從我們的眼光來看，語言科學已揭露了這些名號結構中最細微的纖維，並使我們得以洞悉其演變的最深層祕密。我們能夠看到 *nomina*（名號），即使是最目光敏銳的本土學者也只能發現 *numina*（神靈）。然而有時我們對諸如庫瑪里拉（Kumarila）這樣的現代作者能以何等的精確度解讀其神話的真實含義感到驚訝。當庫瑪里拉被反對者追逼其諸神的不道德行為時，他以比較真菌學家的全然自由進行回答：「據說創造之主梵天（Prajapati）對其女兒施暴。但這意味著什麼呢？創造之主梵天（Prajapati）是太陽的名號；他之所以被如此稱呼，是因為他……」
